topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The campus map felt suddenly inadequate in my hands. I traced the lines of the sprawling university, but my gaze kept snagging on the empty lecture halls, the strangely quiet administrative buildings. Every official announcement, every cheerful student orientation email, felt coated in a thin, brittle veneer. I started lingering near the library late at night, pretending to study, really just watching the security patrols move with an unnerving precision. When I asked a senior about the rumored downsizing, their smile didn't quite reach their eyes; they just shrugged, a gesture that felt too dismissive, too rehearsed. A knot tightened in my stomach, a cold, heavy feeling that had nothing to do with the autumn chill creeping through the window. I found myself checking the posted hours on the bulletin board obsessively, looking for any discrepancy, any hidden message in the faded print.

[story 2]

"So, the final semester is wrapping up," the dean announced, his voice smooth as polished marble. I nodded, forcing a bright, agreeable expression onto my face. But as he spoke about budget restructuring and future collaborations, my attention was snagged on the way he avoided direct eye contact, glancing instead toward the heavy oak door at the end of the hall. I kept fiddling with the strap of my backpack, my fingers tracing the worn fabric. I noticed that the usual buzz of activity—the hurried footsteps, the distant chatter—seemed muted, almost suppressed. I found myself walking the perimeter of the science complex multiple times, my pace quickening, my jaw tight. I kept expecting something to shift, a sudden sound that wasn't supposed to be there, a flicker of movement that didn't belong in the cavernous, echoing space.

[story 3]

I reread the termination notice three times, the ink seeming to mock me with its finality. The official documentation spoke of strategic realignment, of necessary fiscal adjustments. But the way the paperwork had been handled—too quickly, too neatly filed away—set my nerves on edge. I spent an entire afternoon in the student union, not socializing, but observing. I watched the maintenance crew move through the corridors, their movements slow, deliberate, almost choreographed. When I finally cornered the department head near his office, I kept my voice level, but I leaned in slightly, trying to catch a flicker of genuine reaction. He cleared his throat, his gaze flicking toward the main entrance, a brief, involuntary dart of his eyes that made my breath hitch. It wasn't a look of concern; it was something guarded, something deeply held back.
